words: SAM SCHRAGER: You know I was going to ask you a rifle about a couple of your
kin down there at Arrow, who you had mentioned to me and I'd also
heard were pretty interesting people, and one of them was Aunt Susie.
EDWARD GROSECLOSE: Aunt Susan?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. I was wondering what you remember about her. What she was like.

speaker: SS
words: What do you .mean? What she was like?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I had an idea she was quite a lady, is what I heard.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, she was quite an old lady to get around and do and go places

speaker: SS
words: and take care of people when they was sick. Oh, I don't know, she

speaker: SS
words: was just a general old lady of them times.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember her when you were first here? She was still around

speaker: SS
words: wasn't she?

speaker: SS
words: DIXIE GROSECLOSE: Oh, yeah, she, oh, I don't know. Can't remember what year

speaker: SS
words: that she died. But I can remember sometime about a year or less

speaker: SS
words: after we came to Idaho 1907, 1908. 1908. And she was a neighbor what

speaker: SS
words: was it? Four miles from us?

speaker: EG
words: About four miles away.

speaker: DG
words: From our folks, and they had a big family and lived on a homestead

speaker: DG
words: ranch up there^below the George Johnson place down the canyon.

speaker: EG
words: Well, that steep hill out this side of George Johnson's there where

speaker: EG
words: them blackberries are up on the hill and across- that was their home

speaker: EG
words: stead there.

speaker: DG
words: Out from their homestead just below the track, they had a kind of a

speaker: DG
words: garden out there and she'd plant lots of ground cherries instead of

speaker: DG
words: blackberries. The later years that she put ground cherries in. She

speaker: DG
words: was a big worker. She done lots of sewing and crocheting and had her

speaker: DG
words: a garden. General all-around person.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they had their garden out there below the railroad tracks where

speaker: EG
words: all them blackberries is, and they had to pack their stuff to the

speaker: EG
words: house from there. About a quarter of a mile.

speaker: DG
words: Yeah, one time she was out there gathering ground cherries, wasn't it

speaker: DG
words: when she found a dead man?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yeah, found a man dead out there.

speaker: DG
words: I guess he was kind of a, what they call a hobo, or somebody, you know.

speaker: DG
words: out there. So she found a—

speaker: DG
words: Early days them people used to- hobos they called 'em- they'd go along

speaker: DG
words: you know, and anywhere they got tired or anything, they'd stop. I

speaker: DG
words: guess this fellow was sick and laid down there by the river and died.

speaker: SS
words: Did you see many hobos?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yes, in the early days they was a lot of 'em went along. And they

speaker: EG
words: had to bum for something to eat at different places. They was one,

speaker: EG
words: especially- Back in the East when anybody'd come to your place, you

speaker: EG
words: took 'em in the house. Rainy country back there, they never expect a

speaker: EG
words: fella to stay outside, but this country here, why you had all the out

speaker: EG
words: side to sleep in and that's where you stayed. They was a hobo come

speaker: EG
words: to ttoebKcountry here and bummed around for quite a while and my mother

speaker: EG
words: and my brother, Grover, was stayin' at my brother Will's place in 1904

speaker: EG
words: when he went—

speaker: EG
words: You told that before, didn't you?

speaker: EG
words: Not to me.

speaker: EG
words: Did the tell about the bum that had the lice?

speaker: EG
words: Huh-uh.

speaker: EG
words: So he come up there and they fed him but they — but he went away

speaker: EG
words: and then at Christmastime my brother he come up to the rest of my

speaker: EG
words: brothers' up in the Potlatch and I went down there and stayed with

speaker: EG
words: Mother. So he come back then when there was nobody but me and my

speaker: EG
words: mother there and I was just— oh, I was ten years old, I guess, he

speaker: EG
words: come in and sit down there, and until after, oh, along ten

speaker: EG
words: o'clock or so she asked him if he'd rather go— "Well, maybe he would."

speaker: EG
words: So she showed him upstairs where to go to bed, and he went up there

speaker: EG
words: and the next day he got up and stayed around again. I went out and

speaker: EG
words: cut wood and great big two hundred pound fella, he stayed there and

speaker: EG
words: watched me cut wood, never offered to cut any. She got to watchin'

speaker: EG
words: out the door to see what was going on. While I was cuttin1 wood he

speaker: EG
words: was standing there with his shirt open— doing like that— she seen

speaker: EG
words: then he was lousy. She knowed what she was up for. So he stayed

speaker: EG
words: there til she sant me with a note up to a neighbor, Hickman, up there

speaker: EG
words: to have him come down and tell him to leave. She wrote in the note

speaker: EG
words: that Lazarus was there and she thought he'd been dead three days and

speaker: EG
words: wanted Eli to come down and take care of him, bury him or something!

speaker: EG
words: But while I was gone up there, why, Grover and my other brother Hughes

speaker: EG
words: they come down, and so they was ahavin' quite an argument when I got

speaker: EG
words: back down there. So they gave him the dinner- he was getting kind a

speaker: EG
words: fcranky with Hughes but he never would argue with Grover. And Grover'd

speaker: EG
words: get kinda fcranky with him. He said','1 was at your place a while back

speaker: EG
words: and you and I was pretty good friends." But he went away and he never

speaker: EG
words: come back. We had to clean up them lice! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: How long did he stay?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, he come there sometime along about toward noon sometime one day

speaker: EG
words: and stayed til afternoon the next before he left. They had to invite

speaker: EG
words: him to leave before he went.

speaker: SS
words: Your mother didnjt feel she could just tell him to get out?

speaker: EG
words: No. I don^t know what a little, old woman thought in them days if a

speaker: EG
words: a big, husky guy come along and stopped thataway.

speaker: DG
words: Back to Aunt Susan, why, she always was busy as long as she lived and

speaker: DG
words: I could remember, she'd work all day and then of an even

speaker: DG
words: ing, why, she'd sit down and she always had her mending basket or

speaker: DG
words: piecing quilts or crocheting, fixing, sewing, making something for

speaker: DG
words: some child or something that way. And so, I don't know, she finally

speaker: DG
words: broke her hip and was in a wheelchair for a while. And so, you'd go

speaker: DG
words: m, why, she was never idle ft minute, no matter what. She'd have three

speaker: DG
words: or four crochet things workin* 01?. She'd get kind of tired of one

speaker: DG
words: pattern and fool with another one. And I used to always tell her that

speaker: DG
words: if I ever got to heaven that I'd sure glance around and if I saw some

speaker: DG
words: body over in the corner with their hands aflyin' I'd go to the corner

speaker: DG
words: because I'd know it was her,K busy all the time. And this one

speaker: DG
words: time my mother was quite ill— remember she had pneumonia? But any

speaker: DG
words: way they was settin' up with her at night, and she'd work all day and

speaker: DG
words: get on a horse and ride down and set up with my mother all night and

speaker: DG
words: then in the morning get on her horse and go home and see about her

speaker: DG
words: family. And one time my dad brought the horse out of the barn, you

speaker: DG
words: know, for her to get on to go home and he was aholdin' the horse, and

speaker: DG
words: it was on a kind of a sidehill like, and it wasn't too big of a horse,

speaker: DG
words: and she was pretty active lady at that time, so she grabbed ahold of

speaker: DG
words: the saddle horn, you know-, and give a jump and jump up into the saddle

speaker: DG
words: and she^went clear over the horse hanging onto the saddlehorn and she

speaker: DG
words: jumped and she went clear on the other side instead of landing in the

speaker: DG
words: saddle.

speaker: SS
words: She get hurt at all?

speaker: DG
words: No hurt her it: kinda twisted her arm a little hangin' on

speaker: DG
words: but she just laughed. She was always quite jolly and jokey about

speaker: DG
words: everything. She had a daughter and everything and oh, we kids would go

speaker: DG
words: there quite a lot when we were along about twelve-fourteen in there

speaker: DG
words: and her daughter fcjia.. Mae was beginning to learn to sew a little bit.

speaker: DG
words: And so she was making a nightgown and her mother offered to help her but

speaker: DG
words: g-fftfcMae decided that she didn't want any help, she wanted to do it all

speaker: DG
words: by herself and she did just fine until it come to making fes^ buttonholes

speaker: DG
words: and so she put her buttons on and made her buttonholes, and so, her

speaker: DG
words: mothere laughed so about it that she got the gown out and showed us when

speaker: DG
words: we went to visit, and instead of folding the cloth and just cuttin' a

speaker: DG
words: little lit, you know to make the buttonholes, why, cut them

speaker: DG
words: in round circles for her buttonholes and she worked little round

speaker: DG
words: circles and her mother just laughed and laughed over that, showed every

speaker: DG
words: body that come  buttonholes.

speaker: SS
words: Was  embarrassed by that, do you think? The girl would be em

speaker: SS
words: barrassed by that.

speaker: DG
words: Well, it didn't bother ^KMae too much, you know, but her mother just

speaker: DG
words: got such a bang out of it all.

speaker: SS
words: Was Aunt Susan called aunt by everybody or just by her nephews? She

speaker: SS
words: didn't go by that name?

speaker: DG
words: Well, everybody called her Aunt Susan. There was so many Grosecloses

speaker: DG
words: around, you know. It was Aunt Susan and Uncle Jim and Aunt Lizy and

speaker: DG
words: Big Jim and Little Jim, and all of that. But my brothers and all, when

speaker: DG
words: they were little, why they liked to go and visit Aunt Susan and

speaker: DG
words: Uncle Jim, and when they were small they'd say, "Let's go to Susan-Jim's."

speaker: DG
words: And they made the folks to go up to Jim's and stay all day- Susan-Jim's.

speaker: DG
words: "Want to go to Susan-Jim's." But Aunt Susan was just aunt to the. neices

speaker: DG
words: and to their nephews and everybody that come along;\Aunt Susan, yo u

speaker: DG
words: know.

speaker: DG
words: Why would they want to spend the day at her place?

speaker: DG
words: Well, they had kids that were smallt you know, and just jolly friends.

speaker: DG
words: And everything, you know.

speaker: SS
words: But she worked harder than the average woman?

speaker: DG
words: Well, I don't know that she actually did. They all had a lot of families,

speaker: DG
words: but I don t think she was a harder worker than any of.the other that did

speaker: DG
words: that.

speaker: EG
words: Well, I don't know, she was husky along about that time than the others

speaker: EG
words: around. She had that family of nine to look after. Well, I don't know,

speaker: EG
words: after I growed up I said that Aunt Susan and my mother was two of the

speaker: EG
words: meanest women that ever lived. They raised a whole bunch of boys that

speaker: EG
words: dddk ?t know anything. We didn't know anything about housework, we nev

speaker: EG
words: er had to wash fchhe dishes, we never cleaned up the house. We didn't

speaker: EG
words: know nothin' about that. No. Our mothers done that. Had some cousins

speaker: EG
words: that come down here from Spokane and they had nine kids, too, but the

speaker: EG
words: help little boys, when they got to be eight-nine years old, they to

speaker: EG
words: do the dishes. The older boys- well,the oldest one he was a bellhop

speaker: EG
words: from Spokane, the folks was away and the first thing he done before he

speaker: EG
words: got dinner was to go and take his broom and clean the whole house, clean

speaker: EG
words: it all up good, and then he could make a good dinner. I seen. ... there-

speaker: EG
words: Three girls. them people learnt their boys something, mother and Aunt Susan they

speaker: EG
words: just waited on on us boys like, I don't know what they did like.

speaker: SS
words: What about the girls? Did the girls work?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yeah, the girls, they worked, they done housework.

speaker: DG
words: He only had one sister himself.

speaker: EG
words: I only had one.

speaker: DG
words: But Aunt Susan had four girls, I think.

speaker: EG
words: Three.

speaker: SS
words: Now, you must have enjoyed that when you were growing up, not fo have

speaker: SS
words: to do any of that stuff.

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yeah. But when we got a little older and had to get out on our own

speaker: EG
words: a little bit, it wasn't so good.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't know how to bach?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. I went out, well, when I was seventeen, my sister, was'sixteen,

speaker: EG
words: we went up in the hills and milked cows. Sent all the cattle up here in

speaker: EG
words: the hills from Tftkean, seventeen Iwent up there and my sis

speaker: EG
words: ter got a toothache. It was a filled tooth, ulcerated, and there was

speaker: EG
words: nothing that would ease it but cold water in her mouth. You know how

speaker: EG
words: long that would ease it. That was all she could find. Well, she started

speaker: EG
words: out the next day with my brother, a preacher, he had to

speaker: EG
words: come out to preach, so they thought they could get a tooth pulled at

speaker: EG
words: Southwick; there was nobody there and *he -had to go clear to Kendrick.

speaker: EG
words: Then she was down in this country and when she got down here then she

speaker: EG
words: stayed down here two weeks. I was up there alone, and see I had eleven

speaker: EG
words: cows to milk and ten calves to feed and a separator to clean up and do

speaker: EG
words: my own cookin'; carry^water that I drunk and washed stuff up three

speaker: EG
words: quarters of a mile in a bucket for two weeks, and bring in my cows. Oh,

speaker: EG
words: I knowed how to stir up bread, but one batch, the last batch I made, I

speaker: EG
words: forgot to put any salt in it. And I would get up of a morning and go

speaker: EG
words: and milk, take the cows and get them out; tend to the calves and then

speaker: EG
words: I'd get my breakfast over about ten o'clock. Get things cleaned up

speaker: EG
words: about ten and then lay down and sleep til about three. Get up and go

speaker: EG
words: and out after the cows. There was no fences, you pointed the cows north

speaker: EG
words: and you'd expect to go north to find 'em and if they cirlced and went

speaker: EG
words: south, maybe you didn't find 'em for a day or two. So this other even

speaker: EG
words: ing I come in and my oldest brother and his wife and my sister come in

speaker: EG
words: with a four horse team. My brother got sick and my sister had to drive

speaker: EG
words: the team in; she hadn't been used to drivin' four horses; she was good

speaker: EG
words: with two. She come in with that outfit. I got in and I had nothin' to

speaker: EG
words: eat, they'd eaten everything up. My sister-in-law she said that was the

speaker: EG
words: best bread she'd ever eat. Well, she'd been out all day with^nothin'

speaker: EG
words: to eat. And I Laughed at her, I told her that I could beat her cookin'!

speaker: EG
words: She stayed there for a couple of weeks, and boy, if she didn't do her

speaker: EG
words: best. She put cream in her biscuits. She had biscuits you could hard

speaker: EG
words: ly pick up, they'd pretty near fall to pieces. But I never would brag

speaker: EG
words: on her, only, "That was pretty good."

speaker: SS
words: She was doing that because you had been doing all the cookin' by your

speaker: SS
words: self?

speaker: EG
words: I had been doing thgiycookin' and she wanted me to brag on her cookin'.

speaker: EG
words: She could cook, alright, but then she could slop it up, too, if she

speaker: EG
words: wanted to. Didn t try all the time, but that time she was doin' her

speaker: EG
words: best.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think the idea was that your mother and pur aunt didn't

speaker: SS
words: teach the boys?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, I don't know, I guess back up to their time that was the way it was

speaker: EG
words: done, and they was a little older than this other family. And this

speaker: EG
words: other family was raised in town. I guess that's the way all old-timers

speaker: EG
words: done, the men done work outside the house and the women done the

speaker: EG
words: a that

speaker: EG
words: inside. If they didn't have enough to do,, they'd get a hired girl to

speaker: EG
words: help. Men didn't do much, I guess, only when they had to go out in the

speaker: EG
words: hills huntin1, or somewhere like that.

speaker: DG
words: Women didn'tjgo out and help in the fields, you know, like they did in

speaker: DG
words: later years. You know, they'd maybe spin and weave and sew and cook

speaker: DG
words: Yeah, they did£ My mother used to go out in the fields and beans.

speaker: SS
words: When she was young?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, well, she was young, yes, it was after I could remember though-

speaker: EG
words: I was the youngest one, she wasn't very young.

speaker: SS
words: What you're sayin'—

speaker: DG
words: Well, my folks, I can't remember my aunts and all of them, you know

speaker: DG
words: and Granddad's family doing very much outside work, but they would

speaker: DG
words: help to gather in the fruit or something, and then when they got big

speaker: DG
words: enough they'd go and do housework for somebody else, you know, to earn

speaker: DG
words: money, but I can't remember of them just going out in the fields and

speaker: DG
words: takin part in everything.

speaker: DG
words: Then you figure then that the ouMe work was more later for women?

speaker: DG
words: Seems to me like it is. More and more women's lib!

speaker: DG
words: Well, what about you? Were you raised to do just the inside woto(, for

speaker: DG
words: the most part?

speaker: DG
words: No, not entirely. My main chores at home though was really bringing

speaker: DG
words: in the cows and milking and things like that. And I didn't do an awful

speaker: DG
words: lot of work,

speaker: EG
words: Didn't do much field and garden work.

speaker: DG
words: I married pretty young, and then after I was married, why, I loved to

speaker: DG
words: work outdoors. I still don't like housework. I don't like any part of

speaker: DG
words: it. I used to like to go out and shock hay and mow the hay back in

speaker: DG
words: the barn when they hauled it in. And I never did do much mowing, but

speaker: DG
words: I did quite a lot of raking and shocking and hauling and all like that.

speaker: DG
words: I guess I didn't understand enough,. I'd run into something

speaker: DG
words: with the mower blade, but I tried it a little bit. Even tried plowing

speaker: DG
words: a little, but I didn't get very far with that because cut and cover,

speaker: DG
words: But I just thought it was fun.

speaker: SS
words: Because what?

speaker: DG
words: Plowed- cut and cover, you know, just get in and out,' ' keep

speaker: DG
words: the furrow like he did and Id get real aggravated. When oldest

speaker: DG
words: daughter was a baby I went out, it was so lonesome, you know, went

speaker: DG
words: out in the field where he was aplowin'. followin along after him,

speaker: DG
words: watchin' him plow, and I asked him to let me plow and he could carry

speaker: DG
words: the baby, and so, he let me try, you know. And I would get pretty ag

speaker: DG
words: gravated because I couldn't do it as good as he could! (Chuckles)

speaker: EG
words: Well, that's a funny thing about them old footburner plows. A man

speaker: EG
words: that was used to runnin' 'em, you see, he knows that every move that

speaker: EG
words: plow makes, and it's just adjusted like that, you know, but the first

speaker: EG
words: time you start to plow, if that plow starts to go too far, take too

speaker: EG
words: much land, well, you don't notice it until it's got over there four

speaker: EG
words: or five inches then you throw it back and when you throw it bac, most

speaker: EG
words: likely you'll throw it too far, and it'11 be going this waywhen

speaker: EG
words: it goes this far it won't cut it all out and just throw it over

speaker: EG
words: and that's what they call cuttin' and covering when you don't do it,

speaker: EG
words: and then you press down the handle a little too much, it'll come up and

speaker: EG
words: make dog beds and-

speaker: SS
words: Dog beds?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, dog beds in there- the plow don't run level, why, it'll go up and

speaker: EG
words: down you know, and make a deep place and a shallow one. It's quite a

speaker: EG
words: thing to that.

speaker: SS
words: That's what was happening to you?

speaker: DG
words: Cuttin and coverin'. I didn't plow a very long strip, but walk

speaker: DG
words: along and seeing him  easy and smooth, you know.

speaker: EG
words: Well, I don't know, you never seemed to think after you got used to

speaker: EG
words: tunnin' that plow, if it started to go too far thataway, why you just

speaker: EG
words: back, you know. Well, it was all done within an inch, if that tipped

speaker: EG
words: an inch, why you throwed it back an inch and you held it right there^

speaker: EG
words: And if you had a plow sharpened right, you could set it in the ground

speaker: EG
words: and turn it loose and it would run, but if it wasn't, why then, you

speaker: EG
words: had to keep fightin' it.

speaker: SS
words: Well listen, if you didn't like to do the housework and that kind of

speaker: SS
words: stuff and the cookin' so much, you still must have had to do an awful

speaker: SS
words: lot of it in those days, didn't you?

speaker: SS
words: and

speaker: DG
words: Well, I- when I was home havin' the younger brothers, you know the one

speaker: DG
words: sister,^1 was eight years older than the rest of 'em, and I had to do a

speaker: DG
words: lot of work, and a lot of babysittin' and a lot of water carryin' and

speaker: DG
words: quite a bit of washing and dishwashing and cooking.^Unless Mama was sick

speaker: DG
words: I didn't do such a lot of the cookin', but he was pretty good to help

speaker: DG
words: with the cookin'. And she cooked because we never had a lot on hand

speaker: DG
words: and couldn't afford to waste anything or fix thing nobody could eat.

speaker: DG
words: (Chuckles) But I had to do a lot of preparing and getting things in.

speaker: SS
words: You think that that's why you didn't like it so much, 'cause you had to

speaker: SS
words: do so much of that when you were young? When you were a kid?

speaker: SS
words: I don t know, but I just naturally— Now, my youngest— my only sis

speaker: SS
words: ter- my mother could knit and crochet and sew and all that, but I never

speaker: SS
words: cared for any of that. My sis is like that. She can do all kinds of

speaker: SS
words: fancy work and Icould sew alittle bit, a quilt,  Idon't to

speaker: SS
words: be very particular or anything. I think it was beautiful to see it,

speaker: SS
words: but I'd rather have amagazine or a book or out playin' along the river,

speaker: SS
words: or something that way, you know. I'd rather have a fishin' pole than

speaker: SS
words: I would to have a needle in my hand, or a crochet hook. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did you say to me before that you had to miss a lot of school because

speaker: SS
words: of your mother being sick?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I missed a lot. I don't know if I've done away with my report cards

speaker: SS
words: but I used to have 'em; quite a few of 'em, and oh, out of a seven or

speaker: SS
words: eight month term, I probably wouldn't average attendance of what would

speaker: SS
words: figure four months out of a term in the schoolroom. I'd be missing so

speaker: SS
words: much through sickness in the family.

speaker: SS
words: It wasn't your being sick so much as your mother? Right?

speaker: SS
words: No, I didn't miss a lot on my being so sick, just mostly some of the

speaker: SS
words: rest of the family, you know, helping with the little ones and with my

speaker: SS
words: mother.

speaker: SS
words: Do you feel that that retarded your learning?

speaker: DG
words: What?

speaker: DG
words: Do you think that held back your learning a lot to miss too much school?

speaker: DG
words: Could you catch up and that kind of thing easy?

speaker: DG
words: Well, I could do pretty good. I got along good with school. I liked

speaker: DG
words: school, and even some of the teachers tried to encourage me to go on

speaker: DG
words: with school, but financial wise and the folks would have had to let me

speaker: DG
words: stay away from home and all, and they didn't agree to it, but I did

speaker: DG
words: manage to make my grade each year. I made a grade^a year in spite of

speaker: DG
words: it all, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember how you felt about that then? I mean, did you feel that

speaker: SS
words: you were really missing out to not be able to go to school all the time?

speaker: DG
words: Well, I did feel bad that I couldn't go. I don't remember that I really

speaker: DG
words: felt that I was being held back. Someway I could understand that I was

speaker: DG
words: needed, but I liked to go, -ift it made me feel real bad not to be able to

speaker: DG
words: go more. To be in school. But, I don't know, I I more or less

speaker: DG
words: accepted it as something that had to be. always kind of felt that I

speaker: DG
words: did. And when I look back on the things that I did, why, I learned an

speaker: DG
words: awful lot of lessons, and quite a bit that helped me after I was married

speaker: DG
words: and.life went on. And being poor people, when I look back on it,

speaker: DG
words: why, I think.I learned something from all of it, that was — been helpful

speaker: DG
words: in my years later.

speaker: SS
words: When you say you learned something, then you think of it like you learned

speaker: SS
words: how to care for people?

speaker: DG
words: Well, yeah more understanding of people and how to be content— to get

speaker: DG
words: along with what you have and things like that, you know. And just kinda

speaker: DG
words: accept what comes and not expect to be a millionaire when you had only(Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Was your mother sick chronically?

speaker: DG
words: Well, no, not so much. The babies come pretty fast and then she had at

speaker: DG
words: times off and on, there was kidney problems and pneumonia, and just

speaker: DG
words: things that would happen along. But she finally had a stroke, oh,

speaker: DG
words: that was, of course, several years after I was married. She passed away

speaker: DG
words: when she was only sixty-four. And there must have been It hit her in

speaker: DG
words: the morning and she was never conscious til night.

speaker: SS
words: But when you were helping and you were staying out of school, was it

speaker: SS
words: mostly when she was just actually sick or when you were needed to help

speaker: SS
words: raise the kids?

speaker: DG
words: Well, whenever- just whenever I was needed to help with the youngsters,

speaker: DG
words: and little things that I could do, you know. The neighbors would come

speaker: DG
words: in and help what they could with the work, but it was just somebody to

speaker: DG
words: be in the house all the time.help look after the smaller ones when

speaker: DG
words: the dad happened to be outside, you know. It was just that I was—

speaker: DG
words: Well, back then, an eight year old kid can do quite a bit, you know,

speaker: DG
words: looking after little ones from babies on up to three and four and five

speaker: DG
words: years old. And you knew enough to kind of keep 'em out of too much

speaker: DG
words: trouble.

speaker: SS
words: Was she in bed for much of this time when you were home helping instead

speaker: SS
words: of at school?

speaker: DG
words: I was never out so much when she was able to be out of bed. She wasn't I

speaker: DG
words: bed— well, maybe two or three weeks at a time, and maybe in another two

speaker: DG
words: three weeks why there'd be— if she wasn't ill some of the children would

speaker: DG
words: be and there would be a need, you know. Maybe I'd get to school one

speaker: DG
words: week out of some months and then some months maybe I'd be there all b^£

speaker: DG
words: maybe only six or seven days out of the month. All in all, if I had my

speaker: DG
words: record through all the years that I went, I don't think I was in school

speaker: DG
words: more than what would you say? More than half of the time?

speaker: EG
words: Something like that.

speaker: DG
words: You can remember looking at the cards. talkin' about it.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Then after you two got married, you had to do all that work over again

speaker: SS
words: at home. I mean, there was all that work you had to do just the same.

speaker: DG
words: After I got married, why, I was workin' for us and started having my

speaker: DG
words: own kids. And then when I got a chance to go out in the fields some

speaker: DG
words: because I liked to go out your know when he'd be mowin' and he'd let

speaker: DG
words: me drive the team and rake. I went not because he ordered me to, or

speaker: DG
words: anything but just simply that I liked to, that I wanted to be out. And

speaker: DG
words: then up at Tekean, when we lived there, there was one summer that, oh,

speaker: DG
words: there was a neighbor had a job and he had quite a bit of hay to put up,

speaker: DG
words: and he kept trying to find somebody to put it up, you know, so he

speaker: DG
words: could stay on the job and so Ed didn't have anybody to help him and

speaker: DG
words: our kids were small, and so he took the job of hauling the hay— with

speaker: DG
words: the wagon and a team, and I went with him and would help him pitch it

speaker: DG
words: on, you know haul it out of the shock. You mowed it? Did you mow it?

speaker: DG
words: Or just haul it?

speaker: EG
words: I think I just hauled it.

speaker: DG
words: Hauled it and put it in? But, anyway, why, it took us three or four

speaker: DG
words: days.to put the hay in, and we d get our chores done and take the lit

speaker: DG
words: tle kids and drive out. What was it, about three miles to this place

speaker: DG
words: and then we'd haul hay all day. And I can remember we needed the money

speaker: DG
words: so bad, and I think it was thirty dollars we got for putting that hay

speaker: DG
words: in. I'd help pitch on the wagon and then mow it back in the barn, and

speaker: DG
words: like that. And so I was his hayhand through that job. And we

speaker: DG
words: the money and very glad to get it. And I didn't think, really, too

speaker: DG
words: much about it. It was something that I enjoyed doing.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like you really had rough times, where you just really needed

speaker: SS
words: the money that you could get.

speaker: DG
words: Well, we never, had very much wealth. (Chuckles) I had quite a little

speaker: DG
words: sickness after I was married. Oh, I had lots of tonsilitis and we let

speaker: DG
words: it go too long, really, before we had the tonsils taken out, and it

speaker: DG
words: kinda ruined my health. And after our second child was born, I was a

speaker: DG
words: whole year before I was gettin' straightened out where I could do very

speaker: DG
words: much work, and that made it pretty rough on him, and he had to help with

speaker: DG
words: with us the house and sometimes we could have somebody that— part of the time.

speaker: DG
words: Oh, to look back on it, it seemed kind of rough at the time, and now,

speaker: DG
words: it seemed that you kind of grew from it more than you would from having

speaker: DG
words: just everything handy and just at your finger tips, like our three older

speaker: DG
words: children had it kind of rough and had to walk quite a ways to school,

speaker: DG
words: and we didn't used to have much. And while the other two didn't have

speaker: DG
words: much, well, they had much better opportunities and much better chance

speaker: DG
words: than the older ones did, you know, finances. And things got a little

speaker: DG
words: bit better. But the older ones feel that they really, when they look

speaker: DG
words: around at their own kids and their grandkids and all, and they talk

speaker: DG
words: about it, and they feel that they really, you know, didn't miss any

speaker: DG
words: thing, because they felt that they learned alot about life, too, you

speaker: DG
words: know. How to consider others and all. Iknow that whenever they got

speaker: DG
words: a treat of any kind, especially the oldest one, why, she got a nickle

speaker: DG
words: or got acandy bar, or somebody give her one, she never eat it^anything

speaker: DG
words: til she got home and cut it up equally and divided it with the others.

speaker: DG
words: Nowadays, why, kids, I don't know, they're not all that way, Idon't

speaker: DG
words: mean that, but I can see it in the little younger ones, they're run

speaker: DG
words: ning into the store for something all the time, and maybe they're gonna

speaker: DG
words: give somebody a bite and once in a while they'll divvy up but it seems

speaker: DG
words: like it's just different somehow. Ican see it in our grandkids, they're

speaker: DG
words: different than other kids. And there was one thing, there wasn't too

speaker: DG
words: much trying to keep up with Jonses, because pretty near everybody were

speaker: DG
words: in the same boat. You know, the neighbors and the neighborhoods we

speaker: DG
words: lived in, there wasn't very many families that was enough ahead of you

speaker: DG
words: you know, or what you consider real well-to-do, that you need to feel

speaker: DG
words: too out of place. Most everybody was just about the same boat. Willing

speaker: DG
words: to share and so on.

speaker: DG
words: How much did the kids- did they really pull together as they were grow

speaker: DG
words: ing up? Imean, did the family operate as a unit? You know what Imean?

speaker: DG
words: Or were they kind of- I mean would they share in your concerns about

speaker: DG
words: having enough to make do?

speaker: DG
words: No.

speaker: DG
words: Yeah. Ididn't think we ever had any trouble. Did you?

speaker: DG
words: We got along really quite good. We've been pretty proud of the kids in

speaker: DG
words: that respect. And I know our oldest boy, one time Iwas talkin' to him

speaker: DG
words: and Isaid, "Well, the two younger ones, while they didn't have anything

speaker: DG
words: and everything," he was working pretty steady then, and they had bet

speaker: DG
words: ter opportunities and both of them went into good jobs right out of

speaker: DG
words: high school, and so I was talking to Virgil about how it was with them

speaker: DG
words: and how lucky they were, you know, if they had a treat, and the things

speaker: DG
words: that we did and had and didn't have, and, "Oh, well," he says, "Well,

speaker: DG
words: Ma we didn't miss it. Well, you just never missed it, you just don't

speaker: DG
words: know the difference, when you don't have everything now, you don't miss

speaker: DG
words: it, do you?" Well, I got to thinking about it, and you just knew you

speaker: DG
words: wasn't going to have it. And you didn't spend your time grievin1 over

speaker: DG
words: it, you just made-do. And I was laughin' about it; we're the make-do

speaker: DG
words: family, I said, "If we didn't have it, we could do without, or find

speaker: DG
words: something else to take it's place."

speaker: SS
words: Were there ever times, Ed, when you were worried about having enough to

speaker: SS
words: make-do?

speaker: EG
words: Well, I don't know as I worried much about it. I just made-do with

speaker: EG
words: what I had.

speaker: DG
words: There was times that he had it awful rough.

speaker: DG
words: I just had to take it as what I got, and make-do with that. Wasn't

speaker: DG
words: nothin' to worry about, because- in the '30's there, there wasn't nobody,

speaker: DG
words: nothin' to do, and there was five or six people in our neighborhood

speaker: DG
words: down there that needed work, but there was no use to go anywhere else

speaker: DG
words: because when you went over in the cities, why they was there by the

speaker: DG
words: hundreds- that was a lookin' for work! We had a little place we could

speaker: DG
words: grow garden and grow most of the stuff we eat, so we was that much bet

speaker: DG
words: ter off than most of the people. We had some chickens and generally a

speaker: DG
words: pig or two and a cow or so. I got down once I didn't have any cows.

speaker: DG
words: And then I got ahold of two and the train killed 'em both one day.

speaker: DG
words: They got on the track; train killed 'em both. And the company, they

speaker: EG
words: was kinda slow about payin'. I got ninety dollars for the two of 'em

speaker: EG
words: at that time.

speaker: DG
words: That bought us another cow.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, everybody else wanted more for their cow than what the railroad

speaker: EG
words: paid me for that.

speaker: DG
words: least we got something back out of it.

speaker: EG
words: They was so slow that I told 'em if they didn't pay me I was gonna put

speaker: EG
words: a fifteen dollar a month milk bill on that— add it on, because that

speaker: EG
words: was all the milk cows I had. ThejY they paid me. When you got an ani

speaker: EG
words: mal killed by the railroad,you're generally about six months gettin'

speaker: EG
words: the pay, or longer. My folks places down there, the railroad

speaker: EG
words: run right through 'em, and they'd put the cattle one one side for a

speaker: EG
words: while and then the other and then they'd come and crawl through the

speaker: EG
words: fence. Well, there was always good feed on the railroad, and,stock

speaker: EG
words: would get to pushin' on that fence and some of 'em'd learn how to crawl

speaker: EG
words: through and others just naturally get through and then didn't know

speaker: EG
words: how to get back out. That was thejmes that got killed. Let's see, I

speaker: EG
words: think we got about five killed at one time. And then he got three

speaker: EG
words: horses killed and another'n crippled at one time on the track.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find that you could get by by bariermg and not using money?

speaker: SS
words: In the early days? Were there many chances for that?

speaker: EG
words: Well, yes. there was a lot of that you know. Like—

speaker: DG
words: Well, you could trade work.

speaker: EG
words: Well, you didn't know^a dozen eggs— you didn't know how much it cost

speaker: EG
words: you to produce them eggs, but then you could always take them to the

speaker: EG
words: store and trade 'em for something. Generally, eggs run from fifteen

speaker: EG
words: to twenty-five cents, here in this country. But back in the East, why,

speaker: EG
words: they used to go to five cents a dozen, back there.

speaker: DG
words: That was about 1904. 1901. We left the East in '02. Got out here, eggs were two

speaker: DG
words: bits, at

speaker: DG
words: that time.

speaker: DG
words: I can't remember exactly how much eggs was when I come out, you know,

speaker: DG
words: they in 1907. But I can remember bought, what ;caey call percale,.now they

speaker: DG
words: call it calico and made clothes, you could get it for five and ten cents

speaker: DG
words: a yard. And you could get the nicest lace about they had for five cents

speaker: DG
words: a yard, and all like that. And then my mother worked a lot for seventy

speaker: DG
words: cents a week and managed to do pretty well on it, then finally, I think,

speaker: DG
words: I believe the most she ever got was about two and a half a week when she

speaker: DG
words: worked for a doctor and his family.

speaker: EG
words: That was before she come West. She never worked for no wages after she

speaker: EG
words: come out here.

speaker: DG
words: No, not after she came out in 1908.

speaker: SS
words: Two and a half a week?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, back in well, 1900, why, girls would go and work for fifty cents

speaker: SS
words: a week. And a young woman would go and do all the housework for a dol

speaker: SS
words: lar a week, back in them good old days!

speaker: SS
words: Instead of five and eight, ten dollars a day, why, they—! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: But, of course, you could buy an awful lot with that amount of money

speaker: SS
words: then, too.

speaker: SS
words: Well, back at that time there, what they called that calico, was three

speaker: SS
words: cents a yard, so, you see, for about twelve cents they could buy enough

speaker: SS
words: of calico to make a big dress or two dresses.

speaker: SS
words: Did you always have enough clothes for the kids? Or did you find you

speaker: SS
words: had to mend 'em and patch 'em?

speaker: DG
words: When , or when I was growmup?

speaker: SS
words: Well, both.

speaker: DG
words: Well, there was a lot of makin' over and hand-me-downs. People'd give

speaker: DG
words: things— now, my mother, being good at sewing, why, she did an awful

speaker: DG
words: lot of making over out of older things. And Dad was well acquainted

speaker: DG
words: with a lot of railroad men, you know, like conductors on trains or

speaker: DG
words: engineers and all like that, and a lot of trains went through. And

speaker: DG
words: he was a cripple man, too, so instead of sending things to the Goodwill;

speaker: DG
words: never heard of the Goodwill then, you know, really, but they would

speaker: DG
words: sometimes throw off sacks, gunny sacks, of clothes. Older suits and

speaker: DG
words: dresses or whatnot and discards; they knew the family didn't have very

speaker: DG
words: much, and they'd throw that off. And Mother'd rip up the suits, and

speaker: DG
words: she made the boys suits. And I've got pictures of 'em where they're

speaker: DG
words: all dressed up in clothes she made and she made their trousers and

speaker: DG
words: shirts. And so, once in a while they was made out of new material, but

speaker: DG
words: we didn't get anything much all ready made, that was bought'. And then

speaker: DG
words: with us— well, I didn't like to sew very well, I have did quite a bit

speaker: DG
words: of it, and we used a lot of makin' over. Quite a lot of mending, and

speaker: DG
words: all like that, too.

speaker: SS
words: What did your stepfather do to make a living?

speaker: DG
words: Well, he trapped a lot f$. the winter. And at one time, that's 'fore he

speaker: DG
words: was married, though, he had a little store.

speaker: SS
words: At Arrow?

speaker: DG
words: Uh-huh. And he raised, you know, corn and hay and had cows and

speaker: DG
words: chickens and raised hogs for meat.

speaker: EG
words: quite a little business at Arrow at one time.

speaker: EG
words: He worked out in the harvest fields some, during the harvest period.

speaker: EG
words: And then in the wintertime he'd make- he and Ed's brother, Will, worked

speaker: EG
words: together a lot and did reasonably good with trapping.

speaker: EG
words: Well, at that time, the skunks— they was a Doctor Foster here in Julia

speaker: EG
words: etta and he was a rubbin' doctor and he would take skunk oil and he'd

speaker: EG
words: give a dollar a pint for skunk oil, and he'd take that and rub his pa

speaker: EG
words: tients with it for arthritis or rhtMnatism, stuff like that. He used

speaker: EG
words: that skunk oil, so he'd handle all the skunk oil they could get. They

speaker: EG
words: wasn't too many people catchin' em and rendering that out. And then

speaker: EG
words: there was a Chinaman he run onto down there at Lewiston give twen

speaker: EG
words: ty-five cents for skunk gall. So the skunks he could get, he'd get

speaker: EG
words: twenty-five cents for the gall and a dollar a pint for the oil. Oh, I

speaker: EG
words: don't think possibly ajskunk'd make apint of oil, but then it took a

speaker: EG
words: lot of skunks to make—

speaker: EG
words: What about skins?

speaker: EG
words: Huh?

speaker: EG
words: What about the fur?

speaker: EG
words: Well, the fur, they got- I don't know just what they got for that, but

speaker: EG
words: they got something out of the fur, too. And that oil.

speaker: EG
words: Did they trap anything else, Ed?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yes, they trapped- well, along at that time coons were just about

speaker: EG
words: gone outta the country, but coyotes, they picked up quite a few of them.

speaker: EG
words: You know, in them days, coyotes, they had a bounty on 'em of two and a

speaker: EG
words: half, and then they'd get the fur off of 'em in the wintertime. So a

speaker: EG
words: coyote made a fella quite a little money. But, nowdays they don't let

speaker: EG
words: you— they won't let ya kill a coyote hardly.

speaker: EG
words: You told me once that you figured that the Indians got lice off the whites

speaker: EG
words: if they got 'em from any place. Do you think that's true?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. Well, yes. Well, now that hobo that I was tellin' ya about stay

speaker: EG
words: ed with us there, and before we come out here my brothers were stayin'—

speaker: EG
words: baching with a feller up the Little Potlatch there and a white man come

speaker: EG
words: and stayed with them. He come there with some Indians. And then these

speaker: EG
words: white fellers stayed there a day or two, and he give them lice. So

speaker: EG
words: chances are he give the Indians lice fore he come there. And then,

speaker: EG
words: stayed there and he borrowed my brother's horse. Well, one brother had

speaker: EG
words: a saddle and the other had a horse and they put the two together and

speaker: EG
words: this fella borrowed 'em to go somewhere and he never did come back!

speaker: DG
words: These are the four oldest brothers, and here they are when they're a

speaker: DG
words: they had on little older. But all the clothes were made these suits were made out

speaker: DG
words: of hand-me-downs, old suits and things, and then they're just barefooted,

speaker: EG
words: They said the man- oh he was burnin' the brush and the man was workin'

speaker: EG
words: on Sunday burning brush, and then the fella— oh, I don't know what,

speaker: EG
words: somebody told him that he'd send him where there wasn't no Sunday, so

speaker: EG
words: he sent him to the moon. And that's the way the man got in the moon;

speaker: EG
words: he was burning brush on Sunday. That's the old story.

speaker: DG
words: That's the old, old story about Solomon Grunday burning brush on Sunday.

speaker: DG
words: Kind of a, what you call it?

speaker: EG
words: That was an old story. That's the way the man got in the moon, was

speaker: EG
words: that he was burnin working on Sunday— he was burning

speaker: EG
words: some brush on Sunday!

speaker: DG
words: An old fable.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you say in here, Ed, too, what you said to me before. That you

speaker: SS
words: think the Indians were more civilized than the white man. I'm just

speaker: SS
words: curious about that. Do you really think it's true?

speaker: EG
words: Well-

speaker: SS
words: I'm not saying no; I'm not saying I disagree with you a bit.

speaker: EG
words: When the white man come here, the things that they done— Well, now the

speaker: EG
words: first ones that come here, the first settlement was a bunch of outlaws,

speaker: EG
words: you know, said they was a bunch of gamblers that had went broke and they

speaker: EG
words: come here to find money; find gold. That's what they come for.

speaker: SS
words: Come where?

speaker: EG
words: Come to America.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, here.

speaker: EG
words: And there was only one man in the bunch that knowed enough to govern

speaker: EG
words: 'em, and that was John Smith. Well, he went to the Indians an they

speaker: EG
words: give him corn. The first time he got corn for a little- I don't knowmaybe

speaker: EG
words: a few beads; he went back again, and they was slow about givin'

speaker: EG
words: him corn, and he took a gun and went to the chief,ytold him that he'd

speaker: EG
words: fill up their ships with corn or he'd fill 'em with their dead bodies,

speaker: EG
words: and they took the corn away from 'em. Held 'em up. What would you

speaker: EG
words: think of that today if a man come to your place and- strangeryou

speaker: EG
words: treated him good and he went away and he come back a little while

speaker: EG
words: later and held ya up and took everything you had? That's the kind of

speaker: EG
words: fellows that the first settlers was in this country. Now then, the

speaker: EG
words: Indians-

speaker: DG
words: If they didn't take it all, they forced 'em to give 'em what they wanted,

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they took what they wanted from 'em by force. And then they trade

speaker: EG
words: 'em a little something and then the Frenchmen they come and went up the

speaker: EG
words: Mississippi and took that country there, everything in the Mississippi,

speaker: EG
words: that was their's. Well, then they sold that— the United States give

speaker: EG
words: 'em $20,000,000 fer it, and then they told all over the world, come out

speaker: EG
words: to this free country, come in here. And they come; went to takin'

speaker: EG
words: Indian land. The Siouxs— some of the Indians, they might have been a

speaker: EG
words: few of 'em that worshipped idols, but the Siouxs and the Nez Perces,

speaker: EG
words: they wasn't that kind. The Siouxs they had a history— I read part of

speaker: EG
words: it of their's— that they believed in divine healing. They had great

speaker: EG
words: medicine. They called it great medicine. And they were very proud of

speaker: EG
words: their women. The women was the Mother to men, and anybody that wronged

speaker: EG
words: a woman - a Sioux woman - he was in trouble. If one of their own didn't

speaker: EG
words: take and take care of , they'd take him over the hill and kill

speaker: EG
words: him with rocks.

speaker: SS
words: You told me about that.

speaker: EG
words: Well, that was that. Well, then these three drunken cowboys come and

speaker: EG
words: caught these two squaws and kept 'em overnight, and then turned 'em out

speaker: EG
words: and the next night three of these white men was hung. Well, them men

speaker: EG
words: had no business going into that Sioux country. That was a nation of

speaker: EG
words: different people. But, of course, they wasn't as strong- they wasn't

speaker: EG
words: as well fixed militarily as the white man, so he just— well, that was

speaker: EG
words: the way in them days. If one bunch of people wasn't as well prepared
